name: Hemacandra Suri
dates_notes: Dates confirmed by court-records of the Caulukya court of Gujarat — Hemacandra was court-pundit to King Jayasiṃha Siddharāja (r. 1092–1141) and later King Kumārapāla (r. 1143–1172) — and by Prabhācandra's Prabhāvaka-Carita (early thirteenth century), the principal Jaina prosopographical source.

dates_evidence.description: Hemacandra's official position at the Caulukya court of Gujarat under Jayasiṃha Siddharāja and Kumārapāla.
dates_evidence.source_citation: Dundas (1992, The Jains); Granoff & Shinohara (1988, Monks and Magicians).

dates_evidence.description: Prabhācandra, Prabhāvaka-Carita, early thirteenth century, the principal Jaina source for Hemacandra's biography.

core_thesis: Hemacandra Sūri — the *Kalikāla-Sarvajña* ("omniscient of the Kali age"), Caulukya court-pundit, and the most influential Śvetāmbara figure of the medieval period — composes Jaina works across grammar, lexicography, poetics, narrative, philosophy, and yoga. Two works are philosophically decisive in the corpus. The *Pramāṇa-Mīmāṃsā* — modeled formally on the founding *sūtras* of the other *darśanas* and structured as a *sūtra*-with-auto-*vṛtti* — gives the most systematic Jaina *pramāṇa*-theory: two pramāṇas are recognized, *pratyakṣa* and *parokṣa* ([*Pramāṇa-Mīmāṃsā* 1.1.9–10](cite://hemacandra/pramana-mimamsa/1.1.9-10)), with *anumāna*, *upamāna*, *āgama*, and *arthāpatti* subsumed under *parokṣa* ([*Pramāṇa-Mīmāṃsā* 1.2.1–2](cite://hemacandra/pramana-mimamsa/1.2.1-2)), and the work engages Buddhist (Dharmakīrti) and Naiyāyika positions extensively (notably at [*Pramāṇa-Mīmāṃsā* 1.1](cite://hemacandra/pramana-mimamsa/1.1).13ff., on the Buddhist analysis of *pratyakṣa*). The *anekānta*-frame is preserved and defended against the Buddhist *kṣaṇika-vāda* and the Vedāntic *advaita*-position ([*Pramāṇa-Mīmāṃsā* 1.2.6–8](cite://hemacandra/pramana-mimamsa/1.2.6-8), on *pratyabhijñā* requiring substance-continuity against *kṣaṇika-vāda*). The *Yoga-Śāstra* — twelve *prakāśas*, with auto-commentary — gives the standard medieval Jaina yoga-manual, organized around the *triratna* (*Yoga-Śāstra* prakāśa 1.16, with [*Tattvārtha-Sūtra* 1.1](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.1) as the textual ground), the lay-vows (*Yoga-Śāstra* prakāśa 2–3, on *aṇuvratas* and *guṇavratas*), and the eight *aṅgas* of Pātañjala-yoga (cf. [*Yoga-Sūtra* 2.29](cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/2.29)) reread through Jaina categories (*Yoga-Śāstra* prakāśa 5–11). The work also contains a long autobiographical account of Hemacandra's instruction of King Kumārapāla in Jaina ethics in *Yoga-Śāstra* prakāśa 12, a rare instance of Jaina engagement with active royal practice. Hemacandra's wider corpus (the *Siddha-Hema-Śabdānuśāsana* grammar, the *Abhidhāna-Cintāmaṇi* lexicon, the *Kāvyānuśāsana* poetics, the *Triṣaṣṭi-Śalākā-Puruṣa-Caritra* narrative cycle) is the principal reference-set for late-medieval Jaina scholarship and the figure through whom Jaina philosophy enters early-modern Gujarat as a living court-tradition.

engaged_works.title: Pramana-Mimamsa
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Composed in Hemacandra's mature period under Kumārapāla's patronage.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely ascribed to Hemacandra Sūri by colophon and by uniform Jaina tradition; the work is incomplete, breaking off in the second adhyāya.

ascription_evidence.description: Mookerjee & Tatia's edition with translation; Dundas (1992, The Jains).

engaged_works.summary: An incomplete two-adhyāya treatise on Jaina *pramāṇa*-theory, composed as *sūtras* with Hemacandra's own *vṛtti*. The first *adhyāya* establishes the two-fold *pramāṇa*-classification ([*Pramāṇa-Mīmāṃsā* 1.1.9–10](cite://hemacandra/pramana-mimamsa/1.1.9-10)): *pratyakṣa* (perception, sub-divided into *sāṃvyavahārika* — ordinary perception of empirical objects — and *pāramārthika* — the *kevalin*'s direct unmediated cognition; [*Pramāṇa-Mīmāṃsā* 1.1.13–20](cite://hemacandra/pramana-mimamsa/1.1.13-20)) and *parokṣa* (mediated cognition, sub-divided into *smṛti*, *pratyabhijñā*, *tarka*, *anumāna*, *āgama*; [*Pramāṇa-Mīmāṃsā* 1.2.1–2](cite://hemacandra/pramana-mimamsa/1.2.1-2)). The work engages Dharmakīrti's Buddhist *pramāṇa*-theory in detail, defending the Jaina position against the *kṣaṇika-vāda* on the requirement of substance-continuity for *pratyabhijñā* ([*Pramāṇa-Mīmāṃsā* 1.2.4–8](cite://hemacandra/pramana-mimamsa/1.2.4-8)), and against the Buddhist exclusion of *āgama* as an autonomous *pramāṇa* ([*Pramāṇa-Mīmāṃsā* 1.2.10](cite://hemacandra/pramana-mimamsa/1.2.10)ff.). The second *adhyāya*, which breaks off in transmission, addresses *anumāna* in detail ([*Pramāṇa-Mīmāṃsā* 2.1.1](cite://hemacandra/pramana-mimamsa/2.1.1)ff.). The work is the most technically detailed Jaina *pramāṇa*-treatise of the pre-Yaśovijaya era and is the principal source for the Jaina engagement with the developed Buddhist epistemology.

engaged_works.title: Yoga-Shastra
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Composed for King Kumārapāla's instruction in Jaina ethics and yoga.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely ascribed to Hemacandra Sūri by colophon, with auto-commentary (svopajña-vṛtti).

ascription_evidence.description: Qvarnström's edition and translation (Handbook of Jain Yoga); Dundas (1992, The Jains).

engaged_works.summary: Twelve *prakāśas* with Hemacandra's auto-commentary (*svopajña-vṛtti*). The first four *prakāśas* establish the *triratna* and the lay-vows (*aṇuvratas* — *Yoga-Śāstra* prakāśa 2) and ascetic-vows (*mahāvratas* — prakāśa 1.18–19). *Prakāśas* 5–11 give the medieval Jaina yoga-curriculum: *dharma-dhyāna* and *śukla-dhyāna* as the two soteriologically-effective *dhyāna*-types (*Yoga-Śāstra* prakāśa 10–11; cf. [*Tattvārtha-Sūtra* 9.27, 9.37](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/9.27, 9.37) on the four *dhyāna*-types), the *prāṇāyāma* practices (rejected as *mokṣa*-instrumental but permitted for *aiśvarya* — *Yoga-Śāstra* prakāśa 5–6), the *praty­āhāra* and *dhāraṇā* practices (prakāśa 6–7), and a long account of *kuṇḍalinī*-yoga and *cakra*-meditation that shows the absorption of Tantric materials into the medieval Jaina yoga-corpus (*Yoga-Śāstra* prakāśa 7–8). The twelfth *prakāśa* gives Hemacandra's autobiographical account of his instruction of King Kumārapāla, ending with an account of the king's adoption of Jaina vows. The work is the standard medieval Jaina yoga-manual and is the principal evidence for the late-medieval reception of Pātañjala-yoga categories within the Jaina yoga-curriculum.
